They do this very well in China, by the way. Oh, yes. I agree. But, that social media in China doesn't necessarily require all these stupid little ads that you see on Facebook and Google. I mean, because they actually do a lot of e-commerce on Twitter and whatever else, right? Because people trust the system. And so, I think we're going to see much more transactions happening on these platforms and advertising that supports it may be going away. And we'll be, we'll be in our driving car, in our self-driving car a couple of years from now. And we won't be doing anything but consuming content that's sort of holographic or on our windscreen or whatever else. And then along the way, we'll be passing a Pagliacci and we say, you know, I'm kind of hungry. And it'll be aware enough to know not only, not only we don't have to do anything, it'll actually know that we're hungry and we actually could use the pizza. And it'll sort of order it for us along the way. And so, that's where we're going. The stuff that you've just described to me, that's five years ago. I'm not thinking about that. I'm thinking about what's coming next, which is predictive, anticipatory, and not supported by advertising at all. And we're, and I want to say this because we're, I think we're looking at two realities. Where are you from originally? China. Okay. That's why I'm asking this question. Yeah, no, I think we're looking at, and this is my greatest fear and my greatest hope because we're looking at two very different realities. Because artificial intelligence is coming, right? And, and successful artificial intelligence and all these sort of auto internet of things and everything else is going to be entirely different. They're dependent on really good data sets. And China, because it's a surveillance state with people who are sort of locked into these social media platforms, has remarkable data sets. And they are putting tons of money into artificial intelligence. Meanwhile, here in the United States, we're cutting back on university funding. We're saying we don't want immigrants anymore. We're not willing to talk about artificial intelligence in Congress. And we're sort of surrendering leadership up out of another vision of the world that is very American, which is about freedom, creativity, and choice. And we're surrendering it potentially to this rising empire that's going to lock us into systems that we will not be able to get out of. And so my greatest concern right now is that we're not being innovative enough here in America to resist the stuff that we're going to be forced to consume in China. If China is better at self-driving cars and all these other things, we'll probably end up buying and using that technology. Absolutely. We'll be locked into a non-democratic way of doing things. Absolutely. And that's my greatest concern, especially with artificial intelligence. Just like America created rock and roll and Hollywood and pop culture and changed the 20th century, China will bring the technologies that we'll all be using in the 21st century. And I don't think it's going to be for the better. Yeah. Interesting. And I hope I didn't insult your country. No, no. I'm actually American. Okay, good. No, I'm American now. I used to be Chinese, so it's okay. Yeah, but that's my greatest concern. I think we really have to get very smart about the next generation of technologies. And we as humans have to ask very serious questions of this and get control of it before it controls us. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Well, because technology is going to win. Yeah. The technology is going to win. Did you hear the story about how artificial intelligence – you've got computers basically coming up with new languages that humans don't understand. Yeah. You know? And so if AI is doing that, how the hell do we control it? You can't pull the plug on it, right? Maybe we can. I don't know. Any other questions? We've got time for one more. Go ahead. Is that me for that? Yeah, yeah. That's you. Watch out for the mic. Camera. It's actually carrying on on that last one. So I connect with a lot of what you're saying about the current way that we organize our society is going by the wayside. I also read Thomas Friedman's book. And I feel like – and I agree with everything you just said – and I also agree with everything you just said about whoever's able to make it to the game first and most successfully, they're the ones who's going to take the cake. But what you just said about China beating America is at odds with we're no longer going to need that nation. That nation state will no longer be strong enough to make the borders. Yeah. So how do the two reconcile and how do you have – Well, that's a good question. I guess China is fundamentally about – I mean the Chinese government is fundamentally about preserving the party and the nation state. And I don't know if you've heard they're developing this social credit system that essentially our reputations on social media matter to a certain degree. Even if you look at comments in media, people who have more check marks behind their name are more likely to be paid attention to. But what they've been talking about in China is that if you behave well on social media, if you don't criticize the government, you might have access to more services. It's kind of like a credit rating for how we behave. And all of a sudden the government can totally socially engineer all the things that they want to preserve the government and preserve the sanctity of the nation state. And so what that – my view – and I'm still really early on this, but I think the Chinese view of technology is locked in. It's locked in to certainly serve the state and serve the machinery. The American view or the Western view should be about communities and networks and the sense of possible and openness. That is our view of the world. And so that's where the nation state begins to dissolve. And that said, I read even this last week that the Chinese – some Chinese are beginning to sort of push back against this control over their social media. And so what my fervent hope is is that the government doesn't get away with all this and that the people rise up and recognize that there's only so far they're willing to take this. Because that's the greatest concern is that these technologies make people less dependent on traditional power structures such as governments and parties and things like that. Yeah. And China is nothing but a party and a government. Yeah. Very centralized. Yeah. Yeah. There's one other question here. Habsha? Thank you. Firstly, thanks for coming up here. Thanks for staying. And I think you sponsored too, right? And also I have a question. As you were telling about the storytelling, how to do storytelling in one line or when we have to give a one-minute storytelling? What would you say that people like that? I mean, Twitter is a really – some people do it really well on Twitter. And there's this wonderful cliché about Hemingway telling a story in one line. It was a classified ad. It was, "Baby shoes for sale, free, never used." Right? It was something like that, right? Yeah. And that's one line, right? And I heard somebody say, "Ah, you can sort of get the emotions of that, right?" That somebody had baby shoes that they wanted to give away. Yeah. And they were never used. And you can imagine why they were never used. Yeah. That's a great story. Yeah. It's a sad story, but it hits you. And so it's entirely possible to do it in one line. And the way to do it is to sort of, first of all, create the world for me. Step me to the status quo. Tell me that Harry Potter is living under the stairs because he's an orphan. And then tell me something else. Oh, he's got magical powers and he can do something. And then tell me where it's going to lead me. And so with that line I just gave you, it's like, you're leading me along and I actually want to hear how that's going to end. So you can do it in one line. You can do it in a mission statement. You can do it in a tweet. But is it effective enough? Sometimes you do need 90 minutes to be able to build enough of a world to build that suspension of disbelief to convince me that I am now totally in the story and I really wanted to see it end. It really depends on the medium. It depends on what you're trying to achieve and it depends on the expectations of the audience. Yeah. That's a great idea. There's a story. There's a question there and she's one of my former students who can't so I can... Last question. I think she's entitled. You got to catch that though. Yeah. Maybe give it a good toss. No check. So what's the biggest difference in how stories are being told by students now versus when you first joined? So I've been... How long ago was that? So 10 years ago. 10 years ago. Okay. Yeah. I mean, and this is true with all emerging technologies is that we always use the previous primary medium as the baseline. So when radio was invented, everything was a play because theater was the previous thing. Yeah. TV was radio. And now, I mean, YouTube has been... Even podcasts have sort of been using the TV construct. Oh, sure. With breaks and all that stuff. And I think that's going to change. I think what's happening... I actually think even this narrative, this hero's journey thing might change because if you're looking at virtual reality, if you're looking at artificial intelligence, the experience is almost continuous and multi-perspective. And so, you know, movies are very linear. I'm focused on you as a character. But if you have virtual reality, you've got five other things happening and you can choose to pay attention to those things. So it's multiple storylines. It may be things that don't get resolved. And so, in terms of what our students are looking at now that's different from, say, ten years ago. Ten years ago, it was the Wild West. And it was good enough just to be on Facebook and on YouTube because that was cool. And your success was the fact that you were using it, not that it was actually leading to any outcome. And now the focus is really, because you have numbers and you have performance, it's like, okay, if you're going to actually create this video, justify it to me, why? And then prove to me that it was worth our investment. And so it has become a lot more strategic and careful in terms of thinking about content and deliver it because nobody needs more content. And VR had a pop a couple of years ago because it was new. And people were doing VR because they knew they'd get attention. We're not going to get attention about it in a few years because everyone will be doing it. And so I think that's what's changed is that we've gotten much more careful about thinking about how to deploy content in different ways and what that context is. So it's not as easy as it used to be. I don't make video anymore and I used to be a filmmaker. I just find it too hard. And if I'm going to do it, I better have a really good reason for it. And it better be really good too because I'm competing with Game of Thrones and that guy who's making millions of dollars on YouTube. Exactly. And I suck at both compared to them. Well, that's great. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you for being here. This has been a wonderful, wonderful time. I really appreciate it. Thanks to Clark Nuber. Thanks to Abhishek. Thanks, Abhishek.

Come on up, Hanson.
Come on.

So, Hanson, first of all, thanks for being here.
Hey, Mike, thanks for inviting me.

Way back.

Well, I had five UPS boxes on my front porch today, and none of them were stolen,
but I do have a camera to keep an eye out on who's driving.
Yeah, we recommend getting a camera and a package guard, two things.
That's the perfect deterrent for package theft.
Well, so, I mean, you've had an extremely interesting background.
I mean, this is, you're probably one of, definitely an outlier in our graph of people we've had on stage.
I'm not Russell Wilson, though.
No, you're not Russell Wilson.
But probably interesting, more interesting story than Russell Wilson, actually.
You know, tell us just some, you know, you talk about Drop the Mic, which I really like.
You did a TED Talk on that, I think, or, I mean, I read something about it,
and then I saw a little snippet of a video.
Tell us, like, some of the things that, why you dropped the mic,
and you might, you know, kind of give some context to the group.
Well, just to give you a context about this, I gave a,
I've given a number of TEDx presentations over the last few years,
and the most recent one was at Oregon State University in front of 1,200 people.
And I was, I, just like this event, I was wondering why on earth I was invited.
I mean, like, what else, what do I actually have to say?
And so I decided, you know, I'm a former journalist, I used to work for NBC News,

My question is always, how can I serve my audience?

You know, what could I tell them that they would not hear from anybody else?

the thing that you do the most is that you know how to drop the mic.

So I looked it up and I asked them, they said, no, it means that you're, you know, when you're done,
you can leave in a high point.
And I said, you know, that actually really is demonstrative of my professional life.
I have shot myself in the foot so many times as a professional in terms of a career.
You know, my first job was at NBC News and my first boss, you're probably all too young for this,
but Tom Brokaw, who was the anchor of the NBC Nightly News, was my first boss.

I felt that the technology that was coming, this was early 2000s, NBC was not interested in any of this,
the digital stuff, smaller cameras, telling different kinds of stories.
And they weren't interested in me doing it despite me being their golden boy at the time.
So I quit.
And it was almost kind of a spur of the moment quitting thing.
And, you know, I felt great for about a day.
And then I said, what the hell did I just do?
But that began a pattern for me of this idea that I would do something and explore it with great intellectual curiosity and desire because I feel like I'm a Wild West kind of person.
And I don't build settlements.
And I'm the cowboy because it's meaningful to me.
I have a great intellectual curiosity.
And when I'm done understanding the system, I'll start to misbehave within the system.
And I know it's time to leave.
And so that's what's happened.
And so whether it's as I have my own company or I'm the director of a graduate program at the University of Washington in communication and digital media,
I always know when I've peaked and when it's time to move on before it starts to go downhill.
So along those lines of knowing when you peak, right, how would you coach someone, you know, like, here's the two or three clues that you should look for,
at least for you personally, and it probably equates to some of us, I would hope, that kind of tells you you're kind of there.
Yeah. Well, I actually, because I have a lot of students, they come to me for advice.
And my students are multi-generational.
The program I lead at the University of Washington is for professionals.
And so I like to joke that, you know, there's this whole hero's journey that Star Wars is based on, right?
And so, and there's this hero who is called to action.
So Luke Skywalker's called in to do something that he was, he wanted to stay on the farm, the robots arrive,
and all of a sudden he's got to go off on this thing, so he's a reluctant hero.
And so I always thought I was the hero of my own professional story, but along the way in the hero's journey is also this old guy who helps you out.
He's kind of your mentor or your guide.
So think of Gandalf or Dumbledore or Obi-Wan Kenobi.
And so I realized very recently that I'm no longer Luke Skywalker in my own story, I'm Obi-Wan Kenobi.
So my Instagram handle is greybeard and a hoodie.
Which means that that's the role I have to play now.
People are coming to me for advice all the time.
So I had the greybeard, no hoodie today.
And I actually ask two questions always of somebody who's coming to me, whether they're 22 or 42.
And it's not about when they're supposed to move on, it's really like what is their value and who are they supposed to be.
And the two questions I asked are really simple.
The first one is, if the economy crashed or you lost your job and you just had to make ends meet to just put food on the table till tomorrow.
What's the one thing somebody would pay you for?
What's the one skill or thing that you have that the outside world recognizes as worth transacting for?
Interesting.
And so it could be, you know, I'm great with Photoshop, I can make a video, I'm a really good writer.
Just really basic stuff, right?
And then the second question is almost the inverse.
It's, alright, when you get home at the end of your day, what's the one thing that you just can't help yourself to do and you don't care that you're not getting paid to do it?
You know, so basically it speaks to your heart, right?
What is the thing you're most passionate about?
You don't really want the money for it because you'll do it anyway.
Just do it.
And the two never correspond, right?
But in an ideal professional situation, you have both of those things.
You can get paid for the thing that you're really good at and you can own.
And you can do the thing that you're super passionate about that you would do even if they didn't pay you.
And so that's what I encourage my students to do.
And I always start with a disclaimer.
You're going to talk to me and tell me what you're thinking about and all the options you have.
And my disclaimer is that I'm always going to choose the riskiest option.
And I'm going to give you permission for that.
And that's what I did at the TEDx talk too.
A lot of people came up to me after and said, you know, nobody's ever said that I can actually think this way.
And so I want people to have the permission to go do crazy things because it is a thing they would have done
if somebody had said it's okay to do that.
Because, you know, I used to be a war journalist.
I used to run in one direction when everybody else was running in the other direction.
You know, I've covered suicide bombings.
I spent time in Iraq.
I've been to Syria.
I've been in an earthquake.
So it's all that stuff.
Let me ask a question about that.
Because this is really interesting.
So when you're in that, and I'm saying when you're in it, when you're going towards it,
everybody else is running away from you.
It's actually like crossing the 520 bridge in rush hour in that direction.
So when you're going into that, like, where's your head at?
Like, what's in your head?
First of all, I don't do it anymore, which is why I've suddenly had kids.
Because the universe said it's okay for you to procreate because you're not going to risk yourself anymore.
But in my head, it actually is a weird extreme of that intellectual curiosity.
I want to see what's going on that is putting people at such an extreme situation.
And when I covered stories of war and terrorism and darkness, my greatest interest was not in the adrenaline that comes with that.
It was actually to find the light in the darkness.

So, for example, one of my favorite stories that I did during the Palestinian-Israeli conflict, I went down to the desert and I hung out with these Bedouin who were hosting a Jewish family who needed help.
And so I love those stories.
I love the fact – I'm looking for hope for humanity when most of it looks so dire and dark.
Bleak.
Yes.
So when you are in that, let's go back to just that situation you talked about.
Yeah.
And you're there with those people.
Yeah.
What's your goal?
My goal, especially if you're doing television or you've got a smartphone with you, my goal is to get people to forget the technology.

I can feel it when somebody's distracted and I can tell when people are paying attention to me.
Yeah.
That's a really important thing.

So equate that to storytelling is paramount when you start a company, in my opinion.
Yeah.
It's super critical, right?

Yeah, it's fundamentally, it's a narrative about aligning your values with your mission.
Yeah.
All right.
And you have to have a really good narrative.
Exactly.
So when you're coaching someone about storytelling, you know, I call it storytelling 101, whatever you want to call it.
But where, what are the things that, what are the pieces, let's just say there's six pieces, what are the pieces, the main pieces they need to have to even tell the story?
Well, storytelling is fundamentally about transformation.
It's about a journey, right?
That's why they call it the hero's journey.
And the reason why storytelling works so well, and it sounds so trite because it's become such a buzzword, especially in corporate America, is that storytelling is embedded in our DNA
because if you think about what a three-act story, all stories, as Aristotle says, have a beginning, a middle, and an end, our lives have a beginning, middle, and end as well, right?
And so there's the beginning where we don't know what's going on, there's the middle where we're at the height of our powers, and we all decline in the end, the denouement.
And so fundamentally, a story has to be about a status quo or a baseline and some kind of tension or change that comes at us that forces us to transform, and we start the story
very, very differently from the way that we start, and the story differently from how we started.
And so when I'm looking at this in the professional context, and companies come to me or individuals come to me and say, what is my story?
Or what's, you know, how do I get this out there?
I say, well, the best way to do it is to identify that transformation.
You know, what are you doing that is going to bring change into the world?

I met with, the University of Washington has an amazing innovation arm where they nurture a lot of startups, and I met with somebody who runs Co-Motion Labs a couple days ago, and she said the biggest challenge that entrepreneurs have, especially in Seattle right now, is that they're really good with the pitch deck, but they're really bad at actually the bigger narrative that's going to inspire somebody to open their walls, especially in very conservative Seattle when it comes to money.
And she said the biggest thing that somebody can do to convince somebody to do that is to say, I know what the future is going to look like, and I'm going to make it happen.

And so you have to convince people both of the credibility of your vision of the future, and the fact that you have that transformative technology and the tension that's around it is going to change the status quo to something else.
And that's fundamentally what a story is about, and we want to be inspired by that, and we want to be part of what that outcome is.
Yeah, so one of the things that is probably missing more often than not is that inspiration.
I'm assuming, because that's a bit harder, at least I feel it's a little harder for me when I'm telling a story to inspire.
Well, it's missing too. The inspiration is the outcome. The fundamental thing, and I think where bad movies fail is motivation.
Why does Luke Skywalker, sorry for all the nerdy science fiction references, but I think it's a good commonality.
Why does Luke Skywalker really want to leave the farm to put himself into a situation of danger and uncertainty?
And if you explain that well enough, if you tell me it's because his friends have gone off, he wasn't supposed to be there, and then later we learn that he was actually the heir to some dark lord.
Well, that's actually pretty interesting. And I want to know how this is going to end.
That is the essence of good storytelling, is that you have created enough tension. This is what Aristotle talks about.
It's basically like a winding of a knot. You created enough tension that you've brought the audience along that they want to know how it's going to end.
And you can't create that tension if you haven't put together a credible motivation for why that character is doing that.

And so when you think about this in the business setting, you want to make sure that the thing that you're trying to build and put out there actually makes sense to the world and that you're bringing people along for a journey.
And when you do deliver that product, it's going to change things for the consumer or whatever else.
And so you have to build that credibility and that motivation up front.
Yes, so it's that up front piece where you have your ducks in a row or your facts and figures in line, whatever you want to call it, that support the product, right?
Well, that's still the solution for the problem.
I mean, strategically, if you look at Starbucks, for example, and I interviewed Howard Schultz, the founder of Starbucks, a few years ago.
And I asked him, he said, you know, when I look at the mission of your company, and I can't tell you what the mission is, right?
You might know the mission off the top of your head, but it's not about we're going to sell a lot of coffee to make money for shareholders.
That is not the mission.
No, no, they basically, their mission is to create this incredible environment for people to come together and to feel and think differently, essentially.
It's the third place, right?
Yeah.
And it's to the benefit of their customers, but it's actually to the benefit of their employees.
That's highly inspiring.
That's motivating.
That's a really powerful narrative.
Yeah.
And it's not tactical.
It's big picture and it's epic.
And that's what people want.
They want to fall in love with these things, and you have to get them with the story.
Yeah.
And the story helps them, the story helps them start to feel…
It builds a relationship of credibility.

If you've seen a great movie and the director has sort of entertained you and felt you feel good about it, you're more likely to watch that director's next movie because they have convinced you that the time you've invested in their narrative is worthwhile.
And so it is about credibility and that relationship, a trusted relationship.
Yeah.
So I wrote a book about five years ago called Storyteller Uprising, Trust and, I forgot the other word, Trust and Persuasion in the Digital Age.
It's like what has happened in the last 30 years is that we've moved away from institutions that have held the keys to the gates around information to everybody being able to do this, right?
Which is great and it's democratized it and Donald Trump is president.
However, or is that but?
I don't know.
That's another conversation.
But what the challenge is is that because we're all communicating, we don't know who to trust anymore.
Yeah.

And so if you can actually find a narrative that is powerful enough to convince people that they should tune in to what you have to say and they want to stay around long enough to see how it's going to end, that's the trust.
That's the relationship.
So it's not just about creating a great story.

It's about, it's actually taking us back thousands of years to the traveling minstrels and storytellers in Ireland or wherever else where you actually are connecting to a community and you're convincing people that you're a credible person to have something that's worth their investing your time in and you actually might build a longer lasting relationship because of that story.
What, how do you, as a founder of a company that you have a product that you, that no one's, doesn't exist.
It's a, doesn't, the product's never, the concept of the product just hasn't existed until that day, right?
How do you start to educate and tell that story when it's a light bulb?
Everyone knows what a light bulb is.
Yeah.
But no one knows what this X is.
How do you start to, like, there's no heuristics there.
There's no...
No, and that's exactly right because especially now we're seeing storytelling communication, professional communication changing before our eyes.
